title: If your system trades all the time, it’s probably trading too much
author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
selftext:
One thing I’ve been thinking about with trading systems:

A lot of models are designed to always be active — always scanning, always signaling, always trying to find trades.

But in reality:

* markets aren’t always favorable
* structure isn’t always clear
* edge isn’t always present

So a system that trades continuously might not be efficient — it might just be overactive.

Which raises an interesting point:

👉 Should a good system spend more time *not trading* than trading?

In other words:

* defining when to act is important
* but defining when to stay out might be even more critical

Curious to hear from people here:

👉 Do your systems actively filter out “no trade” conditions?  
👉 Or are they designed to always look for opportunities?

comments.author: ritteraf
comments.body: My system runs separate agents for a technical, momentum, and macro context - each getting prompts on a schedule and targeting different time frames. I'm currently testing to see which one performs the best
